subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 8! In this unit you will become confident at telling and using time. Time is one of the most useful ideas in all of mathematics because it shapes every single day of your life. When Amina wakes for the Fajr prayer, when Yusuf hurries to catch the bus to Nuur Academy, when Halima helps her mother time the bariis cooking on the stove, and when the whole family gathers for iftar at sunset during Ramadan, they are all reading and using time.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 3 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: This unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, draw the clocks on paper, and only move on when a section feels clear. Your AI tutor is ready day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

Why does telling time matter so much? Because time keeps our lives in order. The five daily prayers follow the movement of the sun through the day. Buses and trains between Hargeisa and Berbera leave at set times, and if you are late you miss them. Shops open and close at fixed hours. A recipe for anjero needs to rest for a certain number of minutes. When you can read a clock and work out how long things take, you gain the power to plan your own day and never be caught by surprise.

As you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and if you can, a real clock or watch beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the hands on clock faces, say the times out loud, and act out how the minute hand sweeps around. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here builds the strong foundation you need for the harder time and measurement problems in later years.
Name the parts of an analogue clock and say what the hour hand and minute hand do.
Tell the time to the hour, half hour and quarter hour in words and in digits.
Tell and write the time to the nearest minute on both clock types.
Use the language of time: o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to.
Understand am and pm and say whether a time is morning, afternoon or night.
Work out elapsed time – how long has passed between two times.
Add and subtract time to find a new time.

concepts.title: The Two Kinds of Clock
concepts.explanation: There are two main kinds of clock, and you will meet both every day. An analogue clock has a round face with the numbers 1 to 12 around the edge and hands that move around. A digital clock shows the time as numbers with a small two-dot mark between them, like 7:45. They tell exactly the same time – they just show it in different ways, like the same story told in two languages. The numbers 1 to 12 around the face show the hours. But here is the clever part: those same numbers also help you read minutes. There are…
concepts.example: Picture the clock on the wall of Nuur Academy. When Idris looks up and sees the short hand near the 9 and the long hand pointing straight up at the 12, he reads 9 o'clock. When the long hand has moved a quarter of the…

concepts.title: Telling the Hour, Half Hour and Quarter Hour
concepts.explanation: Let us start with the easiest times and build up. These four special times – o'clock, half past, quarter past, and quarter to – appear again and again, so it is worth knowing them by heart. O'clock times. When the minute hand points straight up to the 12, the time is an exact hour, or o'clock. You simply read where the short hour hand points. If the hour hand points to 7 and the minute hand points to 12, it is 7 o'clock, written 7:00. Think of the 12 at the top as the starting line of every hour.
concepts.example: Half past. When the minute hand has travelled halfway around the clock and points to the 6, exactly 30 minutes have passed – it is half past. Now notice something important: the hour hand will be sitting halfway between…

concepts.title: Telling the Time to the Minute
concepts.explanation: Once you are happy with the quarter times, you can read any time to the exact minute. The secret is to count around the clock in fives, then add on any extra single minutes. Look at the hour hand first to find which hour you are in. It will usually be between two numbers – take the lower one.
concepts.example: Now look at the minute hand. Count around in fives from the 12: the 1 is 5, the 2 is 10, the 3 is 15, and so on.

concepts.title: Digital Time, am and pm
concepts.explanation: A digital clock shows the time as hours:minutes. The number before the two dots is the hour, and the number after is the minutes. So 3:00 is three o'clock, 3:30 is half past three, 3:15 is quarter past three, and 3:45 is quarter to four. Reading a digital clock is quick, but you must still know what the numbers *mean* – that is the maths you learned in the last two concepts. There is one thing a simple 12-hour digital clock cannot tell you on its own: whether it is morning or afternoon. Seven o'clock happens twice…
concepts.example: am is short for a Latin phrase meaning 'before noon'. It covers the time from midnight to noon – the whole morning.

concepts.title: Time Facts – Seconds, Minutes, Hours, Days
concepts.explanation: To work with time you need a small set of facts by heart. They join together like links in a chain, from the tiniest unit up to a whole year. 4 weeks (about) and roughly 30 days make 1 month.
concepts.example: 365 days make 1 year (and 366 days in a special leap year).

concepts.title: Elapsed Time – How Long Has Passed
concepts.explanation: Elapsed time is the amount of time that passes from the start of an event to the end. If a football match kicks off at 4:00 pm and finishes at 5:30 pm, the elapsed time – how long the match lasted – is 1 hour and 30 minutes. Working out elapsed time is one of the most useful skills in real life, and there is a safe, simple method for it. When both times are in the same hour, just subtract the minutes. From 3:15 to 3:45 is 45 − 15 = 30 minutes. From 7:05 to 7:25 is 25 − 5 = 20 minutes. Easy.
concepts.example: When the times cross into a new hour, use the 'count on in jumps' method. Break the journey into friendly pieces:

methods.title: How to tell the time to the minute
methods.example: The hour hand is just past the 3 and the minute hand points to the 3. What is the time?
steps: Look at the hour hand and take the lower number it is between.
Count the minute hand around in fives from the 12.
Add any extra single minutes past the last number.
Decide if it is am or pm.

methods.title: How to find elapsed time across the hour
methods.example: The hour hand is between 7 and 8, and the minute hand points to the 10. What is the time?
steps: Count minutes from the start time up to the next whole hour.
Count the whole hours in between.
Count minutes from that hour up to the end time.
Add the three pieces together.

methods.title: How to subtract time
methods.example: Write 'quarter to twelve' in digital form.
steps: Subtract the hours first.
Then subtract the minutes.
If you need more minutes, borrow one hour and turn it into 60 minutes.
Finish the minute subtraction.

methods.title: am or pm?
methods.example: Write 'quarter to twelve' in digital form.
steps: Solution: Quarter to means 15 minutes before the hour, and the hour we are heading to is 12.
So the time is 15 minutes before 12:00, which is 11:45.
Worked Example 4: am or pm?
Is lunchtime am or pm?

methods.title: Elapsed time in the same hour
methods.example: How long is it from 2:10 to 2:55?
steps: Solution: Both times are in the same hour, so subtract the minutes: 55 − 10 = 45 minutes.
Check the elapsed time in the same hour result carefully.
Check the elapsed time in the same hour result carefully.

methods.title: Elapsed time across the hour
methods.example: A train leaves at 9:20 am and arrives at 11:05 am. How long is the journey?
steps: Solution: Count on in pieces.
From 9:20 to 10:00 is 40 minutes.
From 10:00 to 11:00 is 1 hour.
From 11:00 to 11:05 is 5 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Read a quarter time
workedExamples.prompt: The hour hand is just past the 3 and the minute hand points to the 3. What is the time?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The minute hand on the 3 means 15 minutes past, which is quarter past. The hour hand is just past 3, so the hour is 3. The time is quarter past 3, written 3:15.

workedExamples.title: Read a time to the minute
workedExamples.prompt: The hour hand is between 7 and 8, and the minute hand points to the 10. What is the time?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Take the lower hour, 7. Count the minute hand in fives to the 10: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50 – that is 50 minutes. The time is 7:50 (also read as ten minutes to 8).

workedExamples.title: Write a time in digits
workedExamples.prompt: Write 'quarter to twelve' in digital form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Quarter to means 15 minutes before the hour, and the hour we are heading to is 12. So the time is 15 minutes before 12:00, which is 11:45. Worked Example 4: am or pm? Is lunchtime am or pm? Is midnight am or pm? Solution: Lunchtime is in the early afternoon, after noon, so it is pm (about 1:00 pm). Midnight is the start of the night; it is 12:00 am. Remember: noon is 12 pm, midnight is 12 am.

workedExamples.title: am or pm?
workedExamples.prompt: Write 'quarter to twelve' in digital form.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Quarter to means 15 minutes before the hour, and the hour we are heading to is 12. So the time is 15 minutes before 12:00, which is 11:45. Worked Example 4: am or pm? Is lunchtime am or pm? Is midnight am or pm? Solution: Lunchtime is in the early afternoon, after noon, so it is pm (about 1:00 pm). Midnight is the start of the night; it is 12:00 am. Remember: noon is 12 pm, midnight is 12 am.

workedExamples.title: Elapsed time in the same hour
workedExamples.prompt: How long is it from 2:10 to 2:55?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Both times are in the same hour, so subtract the minutes: 55 − 10 = 45 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Elapsed time across the hour
workedExamples.prompt: A train leaves at 9:20 am and arrives at 11:05 am. How long is the journey?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count on in pieces. From 9:20 to 10:00 is 40 minutes. From 10:00 to 11:00 is 1 hour. From 11:00 to 11:05 is 5 minutes. Add them: 1 hour + 40 + 5 = 1 hour and 45 minutes. The journey takes 1 hour and 45 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Add a duration
workedExamples.prompt: Basketball practice starts at 3:30 pm and lasts 1 hour 15 minutes. When does it end?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the hours: 3 + 1 = 4. Add the minutes: 30 + 15 = 45. The finish time is 4:45 pm.

workedExamples.title: Subtract a duration (with borrowing)
workedExamples.prompt: Dinner must be ready by 6:30 pm. The recipe takes 1 hour 45 minutes. What time should Maryam start cooking?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Subtract the hours: 6 − 1 = 5. Now the minutes: 30 − 45 will not work, so borrow an hour. Take 1 hour from the 5 (leaving 4) and add 60 to the 30, giving 90. Then 90 − 45 = 45. She should start at 4:45 pm.

workedExamples.title: Work out how much time has passed
workedExamples.prompt: Work out how much time has passed: a) 3:15 to 4:00 b) 7:30 to 8:15 c) 9:45 to 10:30.
workedExamples.solution: a) 45 min, b) 45 min, c) 45 min.

workedExamples.title: Add the time
workedExamples.prompt: Add the time: a) 2:30 + 45 min b) 6:15 + 1 h 20 min c) 9:45 + 30 min.

workedExamples.title: Subtract the time
workedExamples.prompt: Subtract the time: a) 4:30 − 20 min b) 7:15 − 45 min c) 10:00 − 1 h 15 min.

workedExamples.title: Work out the elapsed time across the hour
workedExamples.prompt: Work out the elapsed time across the hour: a) 2:10 to 3:25 b) 5:50 to 7:05.

activities.title: Make Your Own Clock
activities.materials: a paper plate or circle of card, a strip of card for each hand, a paper fastener (or a pin pushed through carefully), and markers.
steps: Write the numbers 1 to 12 evenly around the edge of the plate.
Cut out a short hour hand and a long minute hand from card.
Fix both hands to the centre with the fastener so they can spin.
Set your clock to times a family member calls out: 3 o'clock, half past 7, quarter past 2.
Challenge: add a thin second hand and watch it sweep once around while you count 60 seconds.

activities.title: My Day in Time
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a clock or watch.
steps: Choose one day and write down the start and end time of each activity: waking, eating, school, football, sleeping.
Work out how long each activity took using elapsed time.
Find which activity took the longest and which took the shortest.
Write each time again with am or pm next to it.
Challenge: draw a simple bar for each activity so you can see at a glance which took the most time.

activities.title: Clock Match Cards
activities.materials: small cards or squares of paper.
steps: On half the cards, draw a clock face showing a time (for example, hands at quarter past 5).
On the other half, write the matching digital time (5:15).
Shuffle all the cards and lay them face down.
Turn two over at a time and try to match each clock face to its digital time, like a memory game.
This game trains you to switch quickly between analogue and digital time.

activities.title: Time Scavenger Hunt
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and the clocks around your home.
steps: Find every device in your home that tells the time – wall clocks, watches, phones, an oven, a radio.
Write down each one and whether it shows analogue or digital time.
Check whether they all show the same time.
If any are wrong, work out how many minutes fast or slow they are.
Challenge: find out which device in your home is the most accurate and why.

activities.title: Prayer-Time Timetable (Family Task)
activities.materials: the day's prayer times (ask a family member or check a local timetable).
steps: Write the five daily prayer times in a neat timetable.
Work out the elapsed time between each prayer and the next.
Which gap between prayers is the longest? Which is the shortest?
Add your meal and school times to the same timetable to plan your whole day.

activities.title: One-Minute Investigation
activities.materials: a clock or watch with a second hand, or a timer.
steps: Guess how many times you can write your name in one minute. Write your guess down.
Time exactly one minute and count how many you really managed.
Try other one-minute challenges: hops, claps, or counting goats in your imagination.
Talk about how long a minute really feels – longer or shorter than you expected?

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity helped you read a clock best? What was the trickiest part – the minute hand, am and pm, or elapsed time? What would you like more practice with? Share your answer with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Reading the Clock The short hand is the hour hand. The long hand is the minute hand. Minute hand on 12 = o'clock. On 6 = half past. On 3 = quarter past. On 9 = quarter to. Count the minute hand around in fives: 5, 10, 15, 20 ...

rules.title: Key rule 2
rules.text: am and pm am = midnight to noon (morning). pm = noon to midnight (afternoon and night). 12:00 pm is noon. 12:00 am is midnight.

rules.title: Key rule 3
rules.text: Time Facts 60 seconds = 1 minute. 60 minutes = 1 hour. 24 hours = 1 day. 7 days = 1 week. 12 months = 1 year. 365 days = 1 year (366 in a leap year).

terms: A round clock with moving hands
A clock showing the time as numbers
Short hand for hours, long hand for minutes
Fifteen minutes past / fifteen before the next hour
A chart of when events happen
commonMistakes: Long hand = minutes, short hand = hours

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Work out the elapsed time across the hour: a) 2:10 to 3:25 b) 5:50 to 7:05.

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: a) 15 minutes, b) every 1 hour 15 minutes.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Elapsed Time – How Long Has Passed.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Elapsed time is the amount of time that passes from the start of an event to the end. If a football match kicks off at 4:00 pm and finishes at 5:30 pm, the elapsed time – how long the match lasted – is 1 hour and 30 minutes. Working out elapsed time is one of the most useful skills in real life, and there is a safe, simple method for it. When both times are in the same hour, just subtract the minutes. From 3:15 to 3:45 is 45 − 15 = 30 minutes. From 7:05 to 7:25 is 25 − 5 = 20 minutes. Easy.

questions.question: What does “Analogue clock” mean?
options: A round clock with moving hands
A clock showing the time as numbers
Short hand for hours, long hand for minutes
questions.answer: A round clock with moving hands
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Analogue clock means A round clock with moving hands.

questions.question: What does “Digital clock” mean?
options: A clock showing the time as numbers
Short hand for hours, long hand for minutes
Fifteen minutes past / fifteen before the next hour
questions.answer: A clock showing the time as numbers
questions.hint: Use the Unit 8 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: Digital clock means A clock showing the time as numbers.

questions.question: What does “Hour / minute hand” mean?

selfAssessment: I can this unit is built for self-paced learning. That means you move at your own speed. Read each explanation slowly, draw the clocks on paper, and only move on when a section feels clear. Your AI tutor is ready day and night to give you extra practice, explain a step in a new way, or check your answers. Your teacher will also meet with you about twice a week to help with anything that feels tricky.

I can why does telling time matter so much? Because time keeps our lives in order. The five daily prayers follow the movement of the sun through the day. Buses and trains between Hargeisa and Berbera leave at set times, and if you are late you miss them. Shops open and close at fixed hours. A recipe for anjero needs to rest for a certain number of minutes. When you can read a clock and work out how long things take, you gain the power to plan your own day and never be caught by surprise.

I can as you work, keep a pencil, some paper, and if you can, a real clock or watch beside you. Mathematics is not a subject you learn only by reading – you must do it. Draw the hands on clock faces, say the times out loud, and act out how the minute hand sweeps around. If you make a mistake, that is not a failure; it is simply a sign of exactly what to practise next. Every step you take here builds the strong foundation you need for the harder time and measurement problems in later years.
I can name the parts of an analogue clock and say what the hour hand and minute hand do.
I can tell the time to the hour, half hour and quarter hour in words and in digits.
I can tell and write the time to the nearest minute on both clock types.
I can use the language of time: o'clock, half past, quarter past, quarter to.
I can understand am and pm and say whether a time is morning, afternoon or night.
